tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Search 5 richest people in the world and create Person objects in the database.

Completion criteria:
- 5 Person objects in the database.
- Web search works.

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">

    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

Search 5 richest people in the world and create Person objects in the database.

Completion criteria:
- 5 Person objects in the database.
- Web search works.
<input>{}</input>

result.title: Richest People in the World 2026 | Ultimate Wealth Guide
The World's Top 20 Billionaires in 2026
Richest People in the World 2026 ⭐ Top 50 Billionaires List (May)
The World's Billionaires - Wikipedia

content.text:
Based on the Bloomberg Billionaires Index (the most current data from May 9, 2026), the top 5 richest people are:

1. Elon Musk - $684B - Technology - United States
2. Larry Page - $337B - Technology - United States
3. Sergey Brin - $313B - Technology - United States
4. Jeff Bezos - $290B - Technology - United States
5. Larry Ellison - $251B - Technology - United States

Now let me create Person objects for each of these 5 people. Looking at the schema, I need to create objects of type `org.dxos.type.person`. 

I'll map the billionaire data to the Person schema using fullName for their names, jobTitle for their primary roles, and the notes field to capture their net worth information.

I have the data. According to the Bloomberg Billionaires Index as of May 9, 2026, the top 5 richest people are:

1. **Elon Musk** - $684B
2. **Larry Page** - $337B
3. **Sergey Brin** - $313B
4. **Jeff Bezos** - $290B
5. **Larry Ellison** - $251B

Now let me create all 5 Person objects in the database.

data.notes: Richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $684 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Industries: Technology, Space, AI.
2nd richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $337 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Industry: Technology. Co-created the PageRank algorithm.
3rd richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $313 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Industry: Technology. Co-founded Google with Larry Page.

4th richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $290 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Industry: Technology. Transformed Amazon from an online bookstore into one of the largest technology and logistics companies in the world.

data.jobTitle: Co-founder, Chairman & CTO of Oracle Corporation
data.notes: 5th richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $251 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Industry: Technology. Major investor in Tesla, involved in AI and defense projects.

Based on the Bloomberg Billionaires Index (the most current data from May 9, 2026), the top 5 richest people are:

1. Elon Musk - $684B - Technology - United States
2. Larry Page - $337B - Technology - United States
3. Sergey Brin - $313B - Technology - United States
4. Jeff Bezos - $290B - Technology - United States
5. Larry Ellison - $251B - Technology - United States

Now let me create Person objects for each of these 5 people. Looking at the schema, I need to create objects of type `org.dxos.type.person`. 

I'll map the billionaire data to the Person schema using fullName for their names, jobTitle for their primary roles, and the notes field to capture their net worth information.

I have the data. According to the Bloomberg Billionaires Index as of May 9, 2026, the top 5 richest people are:

1. **Elon Musk** - $684B
2. **Larry Page** - $337B
3. **Sergey Brin** - $313B
4. **Jeff Bezos** - $290B
5. **Larry Ellison** - $251B

Now let me create all 5 Person objects in the database.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.

  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",

</example>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

Based on the Bloomberg Billionaires Index (the most current data from May 10, 2026), the top 5 richest people are:

1. Elon Musk - $684B - Technology - United States
2. Larry Page - $337B - Technology - United States
3. Sergey Brin - $313B - Technology - United States
4. Jeff Bezos - $290B - Technology - United States
5. Larry Ellison - $251B - Technology - United States

Now let me create Person objects for each of them. The Person schema has fields like fullName, jobTitle, notes, etc. 

Creating the Person objects...

I have the data. According to the Bloomberg Billionaires Index as of May 10, 2026, the top 5 richest people are:

1. **Elon Musk** - $684B
2. **Larry Page** - $337B
3. **Sergey Brin** - $313B
4. **Jeff Bezos** - $290B
5. **Larry Ellison** - $251B

Now let me create all 5 Person objects in the database:

data.jobTitle: CEO of Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI
data.notes: Richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $684 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Founded Tesla, SpaceX, The Boring Company, and Neuralink. South-African-born Canadian/American entrepreneur.

2nd richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $337 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Co-founder of Google along with Sergey Brin. Co-created the PageRank algorithm. Born March 26, 1973 in Lansing, Michigan.

3rd richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $313 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Co-founder of Google along with Larry Page. Born in the USSR, immigrated to the U.S. at age 6. Studied mathematics and computer science.

4th richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $290 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Founded Amazon, transforming it from an online bookstore into one of the largest technology and logistics companies in the world.

data.notes: 5th richest person in the world as of May 2026. Net worth: $251 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Co-founder of Oracle Corporation. Major investor in Tesla. Born in New York City in 1944. Owns the Hawaiian island of Lanai.

  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

1. Elon Musk - $684B - Technology - United States
2. Larry Page - $337B - Technology - United States
3. Sergey Brin - $313B - Technology - United States
4. Jeff Bezos - $290B - Technology - United States
5. Larry Ellison - $251B - Technology - United States

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">

    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",

    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

data.jobTitle: CEO of Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI
data.notes: Richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$839 billion. Founder of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI, The Boring Company, and Neuralink. Born in Pretoria, South Africa. First person to surpass $800 billion in wealth.

2nd richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$257 billion. Co-founded Google with Sergey Brin. Co-created the PageRank algorithm. Born on March 26, 1973, in Lansing, Michigan. Former CEO of Google and Alphabet Inc.

3rd richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$237 billion. Co-founded Google with Larry Page. Born in the USSR, immigrated to the U.S. at age 6. Studied mathematics and computer science at the University of Maryland. Controlling shareholder and board member at Alphabet.

4th richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$224 billion. Founded Amazon in 1994 as an online bookstore. Also owns The Washington Post and Blue Origin aerospace company. Graduated summa cum laude from Princeton University in 1986.

5th richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$222 billion. Co-founded Facebook in 2004 while studying at Harvard University. The company later rebranded to Meta Platforms. Meta operates Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp.

Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

content.text:
Based on the search results, the top 5 richest people according to the Forbes 2026 list are:

1. **Elon Musk** - ~$839 billion - Founder of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI - American (born in South Africa)
2. **Larry Page** - ~$257 billion - Co-founder of Google/Alphabet - American
3. **Sergey Brin** - ~$237 billion - Co-founder of Google/Alphabet - American (born in USSR)


4. **Jeff Bezos** - ~$224 billion - Founder of Amazon - American
5. **Mark Zuckerberg** - ~$222 billion - Co-founder of Facebook/Meta - American

Now I'm creating Person objects for each of these billionaires using the org.dxos.type.person schema with fields for their full names, titles, and relevant notes about their wealth and companies.

I now have the data I need. Based on the Forbes 2026 list, the top 5 richest people are:

1. **Elon Musk** - $839B
2. **Larry Page** - $257B
3. **Sergey Brin** - $237B
4. **Jeff Bezos** - $224B
5. **Mark Zuckerberg** - $222B

Now let me create all 5 Person objects in the database:

data.jobTitle: CEO of Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI
data.notes: Richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$839 billion. Founder of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI, The Boring Company, and Neuralink. Born in Pretoria, South Africa. First person to surpass $800 billion in wealth.

2nd richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$257 billion. Co-founded Google with Sergey Brin. Co-created the PageRank algorithm. Born on March 26, 1973, in Lansing, Michigan. Former CEO of Google and Alphabet Inc.

3rd richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$237 billion. Co-founded Google with Larry Page. Born in the USSR, immigrated to the U.S. at age 6. Studied mathematics and computer science at the University of Maryland. Controlling shareholder and board member at Alphabet.

4th richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$224 billion. Founded Amazon in 1994 as an online bookstore. Also owns The Washington Post and Blue Origin aerospace company. Graduated summa cum laude from Princeton University in 1986.

5th richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$222 billion. Co-founded Facebook in 2004 while studying at Harvard University. The company later rebranded to Meta Platforms. Meta operates Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp.

content.text:
Based on the search results, the top 5 richest people according to the Forbes 2026 list are:

1. **Elon Musk** - ~$839 billion - Founder of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI - American (born in South Africa)
2. **Larry Page** - ~$257 billion - Co-founder of Google/Alphabet - American
3. **Sergey Brin** - ~$237 billion - Co-founder of Google/Alphabet - American (born in USSR)


4. **Jeff Bezos** - ~$224 billion - Founder of Amazon - American
5. **Mark Zuckerberg** - ~$222 billion - Co-founder of Facebook/Meta - American

Now I'm creating Person objects for each of these billionaires using the org.dxos.type.person schema with fields for their full names, titles, and relevant notes about their wealth and companies.

data.jobTitle: CEO of Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI
data.notes: Richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$839 billion. Founder of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI, The Boring Company, and Neuralink. Born in Pretoria, South Africa. First person to surpass $800 billion in wealth.

2nd richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$257 billion. Co-founded Google with Sergey Brin. Co-created the PageRank algorithm. Born on March 26, 1973, in Lansing, Michigan. Former CEO of Google and Alphabet Inc.

3rd richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$237 billion. Co-founded Google with Larry Page. Born in the USSR, immigrated to the U.S. at age 6. Studied mathematics and computer science at the University of Maryland. Controlling shareholder and board member at Alphabet.

4th richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$224 billion. Founded Amazon in 1994 as an online bookstore. Also owns The Washington Post and Blue Origin aerospace company. Graduated summa cum laude from Princeton University in 1986.

5th richest person in the world (2026). Net worth: ~$222 billion. Co-founded Facebook in 2004 while studying at Harvard University. The company later rebranded to Meta Platforms. Meta operates Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp.

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.text: I can see there's already an `org.dxos.type.person` schema. Now let me search for the 5 richest people in the world:

result.title: The World's Billionaires - Wikipedia
Elon Musk, Larry Ellison Lead As World's 10 Richest Add More 2025 Wealth Than Netflix, AT&T Combined
Top 10 richest people in 2025: Who gained and who lost the most?
World’s 10 Richest People: List Of The Wealthiest Billionaires | Bankrate
Here's how much the world's richest people made in 2025
Richest People in the World 2026 ⭐ Top 50 Billionaires List (May)
Who Is the Richest Person in the World in 2025 | Loanch
How Top 10 Richest People in the World Changed In 2025
Richest people in the world 2026| Statista
Who are the 10 richest people in the world in 2025? | The Week

content.text:
Let me search for the current top 5 richest people more specifically to get the most up-to-date list.

Based on the search results, the top 5 richest people in the world (as of 2025/2026) are:

1. Elon Musk - CEO of Tesla, SpaceX
2. Larry Ellison - Co-founder, CTO of Oracle
3. Jeff Bezos - Founder of Amazon
4. Mark Zuckerberg - Founder of Meta Platforms
5. Bernard Arnault - Chairman of LVMH

Or possibly Larry Page or Sergey Brin are in there. 

Looking at the end of 2025 rankings, I'm seeing that Larry Page and Sergey Brin from Alphabet actually experienced significant wealth gains and appear in the top 10, so they may have moved up from where I initially placed them. Now I'm setting up the Person objects using the schema, populating fields like full name, job title, and relevant notes for each of these five individuals.

Now I have enough information. Based on the search results, the top 5 richest people in the world are:

1. **Elon Musk** - CEO of Tesla/SpaceX
2. **Larry Ellison** - Co-founder & CTO of Oracle
3. **Jeff Bezos** - Founder of Amazon
4. **Mark Zuckerberg** - Founder of Meta Platforms
5. **Larry Page** - Co-founder of Alphabet/Google

Let me create all 5 Person objects in the database:

data.notes: World's richest person. CEO of Tesla and SpaceX, founder of xAI. Net worth approximately $619 billion (end of 2025). First person to hit the $600 billion milestone.
Co-founder, chairman and chief technology officer of Oracle. Briefly surpassed Elon Musk as the world's richest person in September 2025. Owns nearly all of the Hawaiian island of Lana'i.
Founder of Amazon. His net worth continued to grow in 2025 driven by stabilising AWS margins and healthier retail cash flows.
Founder of Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook). Meta owns Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. Net worth rose steadily in 2025 as advertising efficiency improved and AI spending stayed disciplined.
Co-founder of Alphabet (Google). Saw sharp gains in 2025 as Alphabet was the top-performing Magnificent 7 stock with gains of 65.2%. Moved up four places in the richest people rankings.

result.notes: World's richest person. CEO of Tesla and SpaceX, founder of xAI. Net worth approximately $619 billion (end of 2025). First person to hit the $600 billion milestone.
Co-founder, chairman and chief technology officer of Oracle. Briefly surpassed Elon Musk as the world's richest person in September 2025. Owns nearly all of the Hawaiian island of Lana'i.
Founder of Amazon. His net worth continued to grow in 2025 driven by stabilising AWS margins and healthier retail cash flows.
Founder of Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook). Meta owns Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. Net worth rose steadily in 2025 as advertising efficiency improved and AI spending stayed disciplined.
Co-founder of Alphabet (Google). Saw sharp gains in 2025 as Alphabet was the top-performing Magnificent 7 stock with gains of 65.2%. Moved up four places in the richest people rankings.

content.text: All 5 Person objects have been successfully created. Let me complete the job.

params.success:
Successfully searched the web for the 5 richest people in the world and created 5 Person objects in the database:

1. **Elon Musk** - CEO of Tesla/SpaceX (World's #1 richest person)
2. **Larry Ellison** - Co-founder, Chairman & CTO of Oracle (#2)
3. **Jeff Bezos** - Founder & Executive Chairman of Amazon (#3)
4. **Mark Zuckerberg** - Founder & CEO of Meta Platforms (#4)
5. **Larry Page** - Co-founder of Alphabet/Google (#5)

Each person was created with their full name, job title, and notes about their wealth and key business activities.

Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

</blueprint>

- Web search works.

content.text:
The user wants me to search for the 5 richest people in the world and create Person objects in the database. I need to:

1. Enable the Web Search blueprint to search the web
2. Enable the Database blueprint (already enabled it seems, but let me check)
3. Search for the 5 richest people
4. Create Person objects

Let me start by querying blueprints and enabling the necessary ones.

result.description: Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.

result.description: Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database.

enabled.name: Web Search

content.text:
Now I need to:
1. Search for the 5 richest people in the world
2. Check if there's a Person schema in the database
3. Create Person objects

Let me do the web search and check schemas simultaneously.
Now let me search for the 5 richest people and check available schemas.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.title: Concurrency

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.
